topics.title: Unit Testing with JUnit 5 & Mockito
topics.description: JUnit 5 is the standard Java testing framework with @Test, @BeforeEach, @ParameterizedTest and assertions (assertEquals, assertThrows, assertAll). Mockito creates test doubles: @Mock (verify interactions), @Spy (partial mock), @Captor (capture args). The key principle: unit test in isolation — mock all external dependencies (DB, HTTP, filesystem). Integration tests use @SpringBootTest with real context. TDD: Red → Green → Refactor.
topics.story: Every production bug has two victims: the code and the absent test that should have caught it. Unit testing is insurance: cheap upfront, saves catastrophic losses later. Mockito is the stunt double: when your service calls the database, Mockito plays the database — same interface, zero I/O. Your service doesn't know it's performing to an empty theatre. This isolation is the point: test EXACTLY one thing, remove all other variables.
topics.analogy: Unit test = testing a car's brake pedal in isolation on a test bench (no engine, no wheels). Integration test = test driving the full car on a closed track. E2E test = real road trip. @Mock = stunt double (same interface, fake behavior). @Spy = real actor who you can intercept. @Captor = hidden microphone to record what arguments the actor was given. AAA pattern = Arrange (set up the scene), Act (call the method), Assert (verify the outcome).
topics.memoryAnchor: @Test: test method. @BeforeEach/@AfterEach: setup/teardown. @DisplayName: readable test name. @ParameterizedTest + @ValueSource/@CsvSource: data-driven. @ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class): Mockito in JUnit 5. @Mock: creates mock. @InjectMocks: creates class under test, injects mocks. when(mock.method()).thenReturn(value). doThrow(ex).when(mock).method(). verify(mock, times(1)).method(). assertThrows(Ex.class, () -> ...). assertAll for grouped assertions.
topics.firstPrinciples: A unit test is a small, fast, isolated test of one unit of behavior. 'Isolated' means no real DB, no real HTTP, no real filesystem — replace with fast fakes (mocks). Fast tests run in CI on every commit. Slow tests run less often and provide less feedback. Test behavior, not implementation: tests should pass when you refactor the internals without changing the contract.
topics.bruteForce: No tests: deploy, find bugs in production, fix, redeploy. Manual testing: slow, doesn't scale, same bugs reappear. Over-coupled tests: test touches DB, runs in 5 seconds, breaks on schema change, fails in CI because no DB. These are the patterns that make teams hate testing.
topics.optimizedApproach: Unit test: mock all I/O, sub-millisecond per test. 500 tests run in 2 seconds. Integration test: @SpringBootTest + H2 in-memory, runs in 5-10 seconds, tests the full stack. E2E test: Selenium/Playwright, runs in minutes, tests user flows. Pyramid: many unit tests, fewer integration, few E2E.
topics.whenToUse: Unit test: business logic, calculations, transformations, validations. Integration test: repository queries against real DB schema, Spring Security config, REST endpoint contract. E2E: critical user journeys (login, checkout). Mock: external services (payment, email, HTTP). Don't mock: domain objects, value objects, utility methods (those are implementation details).

examples.title: JUnit 5 Assertions & Parameterized Tests
examples.explanation: @BeforeEach creates a fresh Calculator before each test — no shared state between tests. @ParameterizedTest + @CsvSource runs the same test with multiple input/output combinations — DRY test data. assertAll reports ALL failures in the group, not just the first. assertThrows verifies both the exception type AND message. @DisplayName makes test output readable for non-developers.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner: test TopicService.getByCategory(), test LearningGateService.computeStage(), test JwtService.validate(). @CsvSource for different role/permission combinations. assertThrows for invalid input validation. Always test happy path + edge cases + error cases.

examples.title: Mockito — Mock, Verify & Capture
examples.explanation: @Mock creates a fake of the interface — no real DB, no real email. @InjectMocks creates UserService and injects the mocks automatically. when().thenReturn() stubs the mock's behavior. verify() confirms the mock was called correctly. ArgumentCaptor captures the actual argument passed to save() or sendWelcome() so you can assert on it. verify(mock, never()) confirms a method was NOT called — critical for testing that no side effects happen on error paths.
examples.realWorldUse: Test Spring @Service classes in isolation: @Mock JpaRepository, @Mock EmailService, @InjectMocks UserService. Spring Boot: @MockBean replaces a real bean in the Spring context for @SpringBootTest. Verify email is sent exactly once on registration. Verify audit log is written only on successful operations. Capture and assert on the exact SQL arguments or JPA entities saved.

problems.title: Write Tests for a BankAccount Class
problems.hint1: Four test methods: testDeposit, testWithdraw, testOverdraft, testNegativeDeposit.

problems.title: Mock an Email Service
problems.hint2: Use verify(repo, times(1)).save(any()) and ArgumentCaptor for email.
problems.sampleOutput: User saved; emailService.sendWelcome() called once with alice@test.com

problems.title: Parameterized Test for Input Validation
problems.hint1: Use @CsvSource with null represented as empty string or @NullSource.
problems.hint2: The validate method can use a regex or simple contains('@') check.
problems.hint3: @NullAndEmptySource covers both null and empty string cases.
problems.sampleOutput: 6 test cases run; 1 passes (StrongPass1!); 5 fail as expected

problems.title: Verify Mock Interactions
problems.description:
Simulate Mockito's verify()/times()/never() without using Mockito. Build a CallTracker wrapper that records method invocations and lets you assert on call counts.

**Scenario:** OrderService uses EmailService and SmsService. When placeOrder() is called, it should send an email but NOT send an SMS. Call placeOrder("Laptop") twice and verify the interaction counts.

**Key insight:** Mockito's verify() is just counting method calls on a proxy. You can simulate this with a simple wrapper that increments counters. This reveals WHY verify() matters: you're asserting on side effects (emails sent, notifications fired) that don't show up in the return value.
problems.pattern: Test doubles, call verification, interaction testing
problems.hint1: Create a CallTracker class that wraps a service and records calls in a Map<String, Integer>.
problems.hint2: Each tracked method increments the counter: calls.merge(methodName, 1, Integer::sum).
problems.hint3: verify(name, expectedCount) checks calls.getOrDefault(name, 0) == expectedCount and prints PASS/FAIL.
problems.testCases:
sendEmail called: 2
sendSms called: 0
All verifications passed
problems.sampleOutput: sendEmail called 2 times, sendSms called 0 times, all verifications passed

problems.title: Test Exception with assertThrows
problems.description:
Build an `assertThrows` helper method from scratch, then use it to test an AgeValidator that throws IllegalArgumentException for invalid ages.

**Rules:** age < 0 → throw. age > 150 → throw. Otherwise → valid.

**Key insight:** assertThrows wraps the call in a try/catch — if no exception is thrown it FAILS the test, if the wrong exception type is thrown it FAILS. This teaches you what JUnit is doing internally and why testing exception paths is as important as testing the happy path.
problems.hint1: assertThrows takes a Class<T> and a Runnable. Run the Runnable in try/catch.
problems.hint2: If catch catches the expected type, return true. If no exception thrown, return false.
problems.hint3: Use exceptionClass.isInstance(caught) to check the type without instanceof.
problems.testCases:
PASS: negative age throws IllegalArgumentException
PASS: age 200 throws IllegalArgumentException
PASS: age 25 does not throw
problems.sampleOutput: Three PASS lines confirming exception behavior

problems.title: Parameterized Tests with Multiple Inputs
problems.description:
Build a `runParameterized` helper that executes a test case table — simulating @ParameterizedTest from JUnit 5. Apply it to a FizzBuzz function with 8 test cases.

**FizzBuzz rules:** divisible by 15 → "FizzBuzz", by 3 → "Fizz", by 5 → "Buzz", otherwise → String.valueOf(n).

**Key insight:** Parameterized tests eliminate copy-paste: one test method, N datasets. The helper demonstrates exactly what JUnit does internally — iterate over rows, call the function, compare output, report each case. Adding a new edge case is just one more row in the table.
problems.hint1: runParameterized takes Object[][] where each row is {input, expectedOutput}.
problems.hint2: Use a BiFunction<Object,Object,Boolean> that receives actual and expected, returns actual.equals(expected).
problems.hint3: Print Case N [n=X → 'Y']: PASS/FAIL for each row, then summary line.
problems.testCases:
Case 1 [n=1 → '1']: PASS
Case 2 [n=3 → 'Fizz']: PASS
Case 3 [n=5 → 'Buzz']: PASS
Case 4 [n=15 → 'FizzBuzz']: PASS
Case 5 [n=30 → 'FizzBuzz']: PASS
Case 6 [n=7 → '7']: PASS
Case 7 [n=9 → 'Fizz']: PASS
Case 8 [n=20 → 'Buzz']: PASS
All 8 cases passed
problems.sampleInput: 8 FizzBuzz test cases as a table

problems.title: Stub vs Mock vs Spy
problems.description:
Implement all three test-double patterns manually — without any framework — to understand what Mockito does under the hood.

**Definitions:**
- **Stub** — always returns a fixed value regardless of input; no call tracking
- **Mock** — records calls, allows you to verify interactions; doesn't call real code
- **Spy** — wraps real implementation, delegates calls to it AND counts them

**Scenario:** UserRepository.findById(int) returns a User string. UserService.getUser(int) delegates to the repository.

**Key insight:** Mockito's @Mock is a mock (call recording). @Spy wraps real beans. Stubs are simple `when().thenReturn()` setups. Knowing the difference prevents misusing @Spy (it calls real methods!) and over-mocking (use stubs for read-only dependencies).
problems.pattern: Test doubles, stub vs mock vs spy, proxy pattern
problems.hint1: Stub: implement the interface, always return the same value. No Map needed.
problems.hint2: Mock: implement the interface, record calls in Map<Integer,Integer>. verify() checks the map.
problems.hint3: Spy: wrap a real UserRepository instance, delegate findById() to it, increment a counter before returning.
problems.testCases:
Stub result: StubUser
Stub result: StubUser
Mock: findById(42) called 1 times - verified
Spy real result: User#99
Spy call count: 1
problems.sampleInput: Three service calls using stub, mock, and spy
problems.sampleOutput: Demonstrates all three test double patterns with correct output

problems.title: Unit vs Integration Test Cost Simulation
problems.description:
Simulate the speed difference between unit tests and integration tests to understand why the test pyramid recommends many unit tests and few integration tests.

**Unit tests:** 100 tests, each takes ~0ms (pure in-memory logic, no I/O)
**Integration tests:** 5 tests, each takes 50ms (simulate Spring context startup + DB query)

**Key insight:** A single integration test takes the same time as hundreds of unit tests. At scale: 1000 unit tests still run in <1 second; 1000 integration tests would take 50 seconds. This is why TDD uses fast unit tests for the inner loop and runs integration tests less frequently.
problems.pattern: Test pyramid, performance trade-offs, Thread.sleep simulation
problems.hint1: Track start time with System.currentTimeMillis() before and after each batch.
problems.hint2: Use Thread.sleep(50) inside each integration test simulation to represent DB latency.
problems.hint3: Calculate ratio: (integrationMs / integrationCount) / (unitMs / unitCount) to show per-test speed difference.
problems.testCases:
Running 100 unit tests...
100 unit tests passed in 0ms
Running 5 integration tests...
5 integration tests passed in 250ms
Unit tests are ~250x faster per test
problems.sampleInput: 100 unit tests + 5 integration tests at 50ms each
problems.sampleOutput: Unit tests finish in 0ms, integration tests in 250ms, ratio shown
